data.title: Neo-Clouds With Gold AI Linings
data.description:
By Yan Taw (YT) Boon

A new breed of smaller cloud service providers is offering high-performance AI computing at a fraction of what Amazon (AMZN) and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) charge.

Last month, U.S. President Donald Trump brought together 33 of Silicon Valley’s most influential leaders to address a burgeoning challenge: How to meet insatiable demand for data-processing power in the age of artificial intelligence?

It’s a question worthy of such a summit. Even cloud service giants like Amazon, Google and Microsoft (MSFT) are hitting data-processing bottlenecks. While those so-called hyperscalers continue to invest heavily in custom chips, power systems and next-generation data center architecture, no single player can meet the scale and complexity of AI’s demands alone. Instead, tech’s titans are now looking to forge strategic partnerships with a new breed of “neo-cloud” providers.

Unlike the hyperscalers, which offer a broad range of cloud solutions, neo-cloud providers simply rent raw computing power for AI and advanced applications - a business model known as “GPUs-as-a-service.”

Instead of relying on massive, centralized data centers, these scrappy operators deploy their data-crunching power across thousands of smaller centers or regional hubs, increasing the flexibility and resilience of their networks - not unlike high-frequency traders who aim to shave off precious milliseconds by placing their servers closer to securities exchanges.

By partnering with neo-cloud newcomers, such as Nebius (NBIS) and CoreWeave (CRWV), behemoths like Microsoft aim to handle daunting AI workloads at increasing scale. For example, Microsoft recently signed a deal with Nebius - worth up to $20 billion over five years - under which Microsoft will lease computing power from Nebius’ new data center in Vineland, New Jersey.1

Meanwhile, leading AI chipmaker Nvidia (NVDA) has taken a significant equity stake in CoreWeave, worth roughly $3 billion at the end of June, and has also agreed to purchase up to $6.3 billion in unsold data-processing capacity from the company through the spring of 2032.2 (Nvidia is a shareholder in Nebius as well.)

For smaller enterprises, we believe neo-clouds have the potential to lower the barriers to entry for high-performance AI and advanced analytics. Thanks to their streamlined operations, neo-cloud providers can rent their GPUs at an hourly rate between two and seven times lower than what hyperscalers typically charge,3 potentially allowing young businesses to get on their feet without having to make large upfront investments or long-term commitments in cutting-edge technology.

Furthermore, neo-clouds can offer industry-specific data-processing power, potentially giving them an additional edge over the hyperscalers. For example, Nebius offers cloud services expressly designed to support the e-commerce industry - a big reason it recently landed Shopify (SHOP) as a client.4

Yet the benefits of neo-clouds extend well beyond technical agility, in our view. Amid broader trends toward de-globalization and regional data sovereignty, we believe newer cloud service players may be better positioned than the hyperscalers - and perhaps more trusted - to comply with local data governance and environmental mandates, including Europe’s General Data Protection Regulation, Artificial Intelligence Act, and Digital Operation Resilience Act. CoreWeave, for one, aims to build advanced AI “factories” in the UK powered by renewable energy and advanced cooling technology to limit water consumption.5

Over the next five years, the total addressable GPU-as-a-service market is expected to grow at 35% a year, reaching $260 billion by 2030.6 We will be keeping an eye on those providers with strong relationships with chipmakers and hyperscalers, as well as those with differentiated software stacks catering to specific industries. For thematic tech investors, we believe the forecast calls for a strong chance of neo-clouds.

1“Microsoft taps Nebius to supply up to $20bn of AI computing power,” Financial Times, September 9, 2025;

2“CoreWeave’s stock rallies on disclosure of $6.3 billion order from Nvidia,” CNBC, September 15, 2025;

3“What is a Neocloud? The Rise of GPU-only Clouds,” Thunder Compute, April 25, 2025;

4“Augmented Commerce: Machine Learning at Shopify,” July 4, 2025;

5CoreWeave Announces £1.5 Billion Commitment to Power UK AI Innovation and Growth Through Sustainable Computing, September 16, 2025;

6Nebius Group N.V., December 31, 2024.

data.title: Toll Brothers: Sturdy With Reasonable Valuation, But Upside Potential Is Limited
data.description:
Four months after my last article on Toll Brothers, Inc. (NYSE:TOL), the stock price has already surged by over 30% and justified my buy rating. This should not be surprising considering its robust performance and growth prospects when I last covered it. With its well-positioned business operations and valuation, TOL still trades at a reasonable level. Technicals still present bullish signals despite the recent dip and profit-taking. However, upside appears to be nearing its limit, so caution is crucial for those who wish to add more position today.

Why TOL Is Still Well-Positioned

Despite its established position and vast domestic market coverage, TOL is not insulated from macroeconomic risks. Inflation remains stubborn after picking up its momentum from 2.4% to 2.7% and to 2.9%. This may be exacerbated by tariff woes as it also imports construction materials and homebuilding products. This can lead to more cost pressures, which may prompt it to increase its prices. Despite this, TOL appears well-positioned due to several factors.

1. Its target market is more resistant and resilient to inflation. I’ve already mentioned several times that TOL’s primary customers come from upper-income households and even high-net-worth individuals or HNWIs. Basically, it builds luxury properties for affluent individuals. These homebuyers are less sensitive to price changes due to their above-average net worth and income levels. Despite stubborn inflation and tariff woes, TOL has some pricing flexibility to offset cost pressures.

2. Its business has a wide range of geographical distribution. Toll Brothers, Inc. is present across over 20 states in the US. This helps it maintain strong brand popularity and reputation. This also allows it to segment prices based on their respective market conditions. This also shows that TOL picks the right market or states in line with the products it offers. In short, it has a high concentration on states with the highest number of HNWIs or those states with high-income levels per household. It also prioritizes states that are home to big corporations. After all, it won’t succeed in states whose individuals prioritize more affordable housing. It also won't thrive in those with unfavorable terrains.

For instance, its average price in North rose by 10.4% as demand. On average, this has the second-highest unit price among its five markets. This should not be surprising since it caters to Massachusetts, New York, and New Jersey, which are among the states with the highest per capita personal income. With respect to median income, these three states are within the top 15. The remaining states are within the middle, while Michigan is on the lower end. The same goes for its Mountain market with a higher average price and number of units.

In other markets, TOL had to lower its pricing either to boost demand or balance price with volume to stabilize their revenues. As we can see, its Pacific Market is the most expensive on the list. This should not be surprising since California is one of the richest states in the US. Meanwhile, Washington and Oregon are still on the upper half with respect to per capita and median income. It doesn’t cater to any state in the region that ranks 25th and below. In addition, its demand improved amid lower prices.

Overall, its operations remain strong as the total units rose by 145 and the average price still increased to $973,600 versus $968,200 YoY. With that, it’s not surprising that its operating revenue improved by 8.0% from $2.7B to $2.9B YoY. It was also able to stabilize margins and cost sensitivity through operating leverage.

3. TOL enjoys strong pricing power. In connection with numbers 1 and 2, TOL still enjoys pricing flexibility as demand remains robust. Although contracts decreased, TOL was still able to raise its average price. Its North market remains the most resilient among the group. In Mid-Atlantic, its lower pricing bolstered volume. Meanwhile, pricing completely offsets volume in the Pacific market. This can be supported by the decreasing mortgage rate to 6.2% and the recent Fed rate cut of 25 bps. While HNWIs may not be affected that much, these economic indicators may help improve confidence among them. After all, luxury homes are not solely for personal use. They can be good investments due to their higher appreciation potential, tax benefits, and rental income stability.

4. Luxury Market Balances Demand With Supply. We can see the notable decrease in contracts and backlogs of Toll Brothers, Inc., and I think the whole market is on the same page. That way, players can improve inventory management and maintain their pricing power. This is why TOL still enjoys a higher average price in deliveries, contracts, and backlogs on a YoY basis, allowing it to offset lower volumes.

On a broader scale, we can see how new listings of single-family and attached homes started to moderate amid the higher levels of inventory. This is how the market tries to balance new listings versus total sold units. This remains effective as their median list and sale prices rose last August on a YoY basis.

With the combined impact of numbers 1, 2, 3, and 4, I believe that TOL’s Q4 and 2025 guidance remains realistic at this point. The target price range of $950,000 to $960,000 is slightly higher than the nine-month average of $946,400. If we use these prices and multiply them by the total deliveries of 11,200, we can derive its FY 2025 revenue from $10,640 to $10,750. We can also derive COGS based on the sales gross margin and SG&A as a percentage of revenue. To have a broader view, I also estimated depreciation and capex using the straight-line method. So, I can also compute NOPAT and EBITDA. As we can see, NOPAT and EBITDA will decrease in line with the previous three quarters. The consolation is that TOL will remain very profitable to sustain its operations.

5. Its fundamentals are still well-positioned and robust. With its profitable operations and effective management of operating assets and liabilities, TOL maintains enough operating cash flow for its capex.

It complements it with prudent debt management to ensure liquidity while covering dividends, debt repayments, and share repurchases. It also maintains an adequate capital buffer considering its low Net Debt/EBITDA of 1.0x. This means that TOL only needs a year to cover all debt at once, even if it doesn’t have to. This is in line with its maturity profile. For instance, its senior notes of $500M will mature in 2035. Meanwhile, its unsecured notes of $650M and revolving loans with a borrowing capacity of over $2B will mature in 2030. Hence, TOL has huge resources to ensure sustainability and dividend longevity.

Valuation

After the sharp stock price surge, I’m revisiting TOL. But this time, I opted to use more forward-looking methods.

First, I started with the Dividend Discount Model. Its annualized dividends of $1.00 are based on the quarterly payments of $0.25. Meanwhile, its dividend growth rate of 11.43% is based on the average growth rate in the past four years to exclude the pandemic year. Lastly, I got its cost of equity using the capital asset pricing model. Note that I set its risk premium at 6.0% to price in more risks. I computed its Beta on MS Excel using the SLOPE function of its daily price change versus the S&P 500. This is almost the same as the reported value of 1.33 on Yahoo Finance, proving the precision of my computation. Using all these numbers, its TP will be $140.71. This means that TOL is still trading at a reasonable level. However, its upside appears to be limited at this point. If I add a 5% allowance, the TP will be $133.39.

I also used EV/Revenue based on the average home price and unit delivery guidance I explained earlier. The EV/Revenue high and low will be 1.46x and 1.48x, so their midpoint is 1.47x. But just like the dividend discount model, the current multiple shows that TOL is still reasonably valued. Note that the TTM and FWD multiples are both 1.45x. With that, they have already priced in the target revenue of TOL. If we use this to derive the fair value, we will get $141.46. But still, its upside also appears limited. With that, I advise investors to stay on the sidelines for now.

Technicals

TOL’s momentum still demonstrates bullish signals as it trends upward with higher highs and lows. The 50-day SMA is still increasing above the 200-day SMA, suggesting that market sentiments are still generally optimistic. However, I advise investors to be more cautious as the 50-day SMA gets closer to the price. We should also note the price dip in the past two weeks, which reflects some weakness that the 50-day SMA may not have reflected yet.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI can explain it. We can see that the MACD line is moving below the signal line, showing the increasing selling volume and decreasing buying volume. Meanwhile, the histogram remains within negative territory, so selling volume still outpaces buying volume. Likewise, RSI has weakened as it reflected the dip. This is due to the recent overbuying that preceded profit-taking. Now, it starts to increase again in line with the price rebound attempts. This means that buyers saw the dip as an opportunity to find new support and enter.

Conclusion

Toll Brothers, Inc. remains resilient with its strategic business model and vast domestic presence that supports its pricing flexibility and top-line stability. It also keeps robust liquidity while taking advantage of new macroeconomic opportunities for luxury properties. Yet, entering at its current price appears challenging as it gets very close to my target price and limits upside potential. Technicals adhere as they present some weaknesses in the price trend even if the bullish trend still holds today. I advise investors to stay on the sidelines and downgrade my rating to hold for now.

data.title: Tradeweb Government Bond Update - September 2025
data.description:
Global government debt markets saw mixed yield movements in September, with both rises and declines recorded during the month. Beginning in the Eurozone, French government bond markets remained under pressure in September, as investors continued to reassess the country’s fiscal outlook.

The yield on France’s 10-year benchmark bond fell three basis points to 3.47% at month-end. On September 8, Prime Minister Francois Bayrou lost a confidence vote, and while the country has a new prime minister, Sébastien Lecornu, it is still without an official government. Meanwhile, the annual inflation rate rose to 1.2% in September from 0.9% the month before.

Roderick Joniaux, Head of European Government and Supranational Products at Tradeweb, said, "The 10-year OAT yield climbed to a three-year high of 3.61% on September 2, while the spread over the German 10-year Bund widened to 76 basis points at month-end – its widest level in three weeks. The move highlights persistent investor concerns around France’s elevated debt burden and recent credit rating downgrades, with Fitch lowering the sovereign to A+ from AA- and Morningstar DBRS cutting it to AA from AA (high). Against this backdrop, French bonds have become a focal point for gauging sentiment on Eurozone stability, with markets closely watching whether fiscal consolidation efforts can restore confidence in the months ahead.”

In neighbouring Germany, the 10-year Bund yield remained unchanged from August at 2.71%. Similar to its French counterpart, the country’s annual inflation rate rose from 2.2% in August to 2.4% in September. The HCOB Germany Manufacturing PMI for September was revised upward to 49.5 from the earlier estimate of 48.5, but slightly below August’s 49.8, signalling that manufacturing activity remained close to stagnation.

Inflation in the Eurozone rose to 2.2% in September, the first time it has gone above the European Central Bank’s target of 2% since April this year. On September 11, the central bank voted to hold interest rates steady at 2%.

The Italian 10-year government bond yield fell five basis points to 3.56% at the end of the month. According to preliminary estimates, the country’s annual inflation rate remained unchanged in September at 1.6%, slightly under market expectations.

The UK 10-year Gilt yield dropped just two basis points from August to 4.15% at the end of September. The country’s annual inflation rate stayed at 3.8% in August, matching July’s figure and remaining near the peak levels recorded in January 2024.

The Bank of England voted 7–2 to keep interest rates unchanged at 4% on September 18. The S&P Global UK Manufacturing PMI fell to 46.2 in September from 47 in the previous month, while the GfK Consumer Confidence Index fell to -19 from -17 in August.

Over in the U.S., the 10-year Treasury yield finished September at 4.15%, seven basis points lower than the month prior. On September 17, the Federal Reserve lowered borrowing costs for the first time since December last year to a range of 4% to 4.25%. The country’s annual inflation rate accelerated to 2.9% in September, the highest level since January 2025, after holding steady at 2.7% in June and July.

Finally, in Asia Pacific, the yield on Japan’s 10-year government bond rose four basis points to 1.65% at month-end. The benchmark bond also reached a three-year yield high of 1.66% on September 22. On September 19, the Bank of Japan announced it was maintaining interest rates at 0.75%. The country’s annual inflation rate eased once again to 2.7% in August from 3.1% in the previous month.

data.title: China's Flow-Driven Equity Rally May Have Further Upside
data.description:
By Han Peng, CFA, Director, Multi-Asset Research and Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist

Despite mounting macro headwinds at the start of the year, Chinese equities have continued to deliver positive performance. Fueling the rally has been a consumption-driven stimulus approach from domestic policymakers, which has helped restore retail investor confidence.

While near-term flows can provide further upside, the durability for the equity rally will hinge on policy choices ahead. A sustained bull market will require more than just liquidity, but a measured stimulus approach that limits leverage-driven excesses.

The Shanghai Composite Index reached a ten-year high in August, fueled by ongoing stimulus efforts from policymakers in China that have helped stabilize growth concerns and revive investor confidence.

While the extended U.S.-China trade war truce has helped ease concerns, the response from domestic policymakers has been a significant positive catalyst. Contrary to the old “bazooka stimulus” playbook aimed at investment, policymakers have instead adopted a more managed approach focused on consumption.

Thus far, this has included a consumption trade-in program, a birth subsidy, and a consumer loan interest subsidy. As a result, consensus forecasts for China’s 2025 GDP growth have rebounded, helping revive domestic investor confidence, especially among retail investors.

Recent deposit data has shown increased rotation out of household bank deposits (savings) into non-bank financial institutions - a proxy for increased market participation. Looking ahead, these retail flows could provide further momentum for the equity rally, and with bank deposits still elevated, the rebalancing is likely to have further to run.

With retail flows potentially driving equity market upside from here, the pace of the rally becomes increasingly important. While a repeat of the 2015 rally, which was largely driven by leverage, would risk instability, to the extent that Chinese policy makers can continue to adopt a balanced approach with stimulus and prevent excesses from forming, a healthy and long-lasting bull market can emerge.

data.title: Largest U.S. Banks Lead Sector Market Performance In September
data.description:
Although most of the US banking sector traded down in September, the Big Four banks bucked the trend, propping up the market-cap weighted index.

Each of the four US banks with more than $1 trillion in total assets as of June 30 posted positive total returns in September. Citigroup Inc. (C) led the way, trading up 5.1%. JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), Bank of America Corp. (BAC) and Wells Fargo & Co. (WFC) followed with returns of 4.6%, 2.2% and 2.0%, respectively.

Those positive returns did not extend to the next largest asset tier. Eight of the 10 banks with assets between $100 billion and $1 trillion recorded negative returns; Citizens Financial Group Inc. (CFG) traded up 1.7% and U.S. Bancorp (USB) ticked up a fraction of a percent.

Banks between $50 billion and $100 billion in assets fared a bit better. Six of the 19 stocks went up in September, highlighted by Valley National Bancorp's (VLY) 2.4% return. Of the remaining 175 banks in an S&P Global Market Intelligence analysis, 16 experienced a positive total return in September.

For all 208 banks in the analysis, the median return in September was negative 3.4%, which trailed the 1.6% return of the S&P US BMI Banks index and the 3.7% return of the S&P 500. Market pressure pushed down valuations. The median price-to-adjusted tangible book value (TBV) for the industry was 141.9% as of Sept. 30, down from 145.4% at the end of August.

Least expensive banks

Dallas-based First Foundation Inc. (FFWM) was the least expensive bank in the analysis by price-to-adjusted TBV for the ninth month in a row. Its valuation was 50.5% at the end of September and would have been higher if preferred shares from a capital raise in July 2024 were converted to common stock. Such a conversion would have dropped the bank's basic TBV to $9.34 per common share from $11.65 per common share as of June 30, according to a July 31 filing.

First Internet Bancorp (INBK), which had the second-lowest price-to-adjusted TBV, was the fourth-weakest market performer in the analysis in September, with a total return of negative 10.5%. The bank expects to report a material impairment charge in the third quarter from the sale of performing single-tenant lease financing loans in September.

The No. 15 bank, OceanFirst Financial Corp. (OCFC), plans to pursue organic growth rather than M&A. The Toms River, New Jersey-based bank has been hiring bankers who can attract low-cost deposits.

Most expensive banks

The Bancorp Inc. (TBBK) screened as the highest-valued bank for the third consecutive month. Its price-to-adjusted TBV was 438.5% as of Sept. 30, down 8 percentage points from Aug. 29, but more than 100 percentage points higher than any other bank in the analysis.

The second-most expensive bank — Dewitt, New York-based Community Financial System Inc. (CBU) — announced a minority investment in Leap Holdings Inc. in September.

Triumph Financial Inc. (TFIN), the No. 8 bank by highest valuation, disclosed its exposure to subprime auto lender Tricolor Holdings LLC, which filed for Chapter 7 bankruptcy on Sept. 10. Triumph had the worst monthly return in the analysis at negative 18.6%.

Also in September, Nicolet Bankshares Inc. (NIC), the 18th-most expensive bank, announced leadership continuity through 2030. The Green Bay, Wisconsin-based bank has earned a return on average tangible common equity in excess of 17% for the last seven quarters.

About this analysis

S&P Global Market Intelligence analyzed US banks trading on the Nasdaq, NYSE or NYSE American with total assets of more than $3 billion. The analysis excludes banks in the mutual holding company ownership structure and other operating subsidiaries.

Adjusted tangible book value is calculated as the sum of tangible common equity, loss reserves and unrealized gain or loss from held-to-maturity securities, tax-adjusted at the 21% corporate rate, less nonperforming assets and loans 90 or more days past due but still accruing interest, divided by common shares outstanding.
